stories.headline: One dead, eight injured in shooting at southeast Denver nightclub
stories.summary:
One person was killed and eight others were injured in a shooting at a southeast Denver nightclub early Monday morning, according to the Denver Police Department. The incident marks one of the city's most significant mass-casualty shooting events in recent months.

Denver police responded to the scene in the early morning hours and are conducting an active investigation. No suspect information had been released as of Monday morning.

Authorities are asking anyone with information about the shooting to contact investigators. The nightclub's name and exact address were not immediately disclosed in initial reports.

stories.headline: Man killed in double shooting one block from Congress Park
stories.summary:
One man died Sunday after a double shooting on the 1100 block of North Columbine Street in Denver, just north of Congress Park and near the Denver Botanic Gardens. Denver Police Department responded to the scene before 10 a.m. and confirmed the death in an update just before 2 p.m.

Police classified the case as a homicide investigation after the victim succumbed to his injuries. A second person was also shot in the incident, though the severity of that person's injuries was not disclosed.

No suspect information has been released. Investigators are asking anyone with information to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Procession to escort fallen firefighting pilot from Montrose to DIA along I-70 corridor
stories.summary:
A procession of approximately 10 emergency vehicles is scheduled Monday to escort the body of helicopter pilot Nicholas Dale, 56, of Sooke, British Columbia, from Montrose to Denver International Airport. Dale died July 12 when his K-MAX helicopter crashed into Silver Jack Reservoir while fighting the Gold Mountain fire near Ouray. The National Transportation Safety Board is investigating the crash.

The convoy is set to depart Cripping Funeral Home in Montrose at 7 a.m. and travel north to Grand Junction before heading east on Interstate 70. The procession is expected to pass through Grand Junction around 8 a.m., Glenwood Springs at approximately 9:15 a.m., Vail at 10:15 a.m., Silverthorne at roughly 10:45 a.m., and arrive at DIA around noon.

Colorado officials are asking residents who wish to pay respects to stand safely on overpasses and exit ramps along the route, and to avoid stopping or parking on interstate shoulders. Dale's death follows the June 28 loss of three other firefighters — Emily Barker, Nick Hutcherson, and Sydney Watson — who were killed fighting the Snyder fire in Mesa County.

Separately, a new fire in Grand County, the Hartman Hill fire, was estimated at 101 acres Sunday evening and has forced evacuations southwest of Kremmling. The Aspen Acres fire, the state's largest active blaze, has scorched 100,400 acres and remains 61% contained.

stories.headline: Western Slope communities begin cleanup after mudslides damage roads, buildings in Telluride area
stories.summary:
Residents across Colorado's Western Slope are cleaning up after a slow-moving storm system triggered multiple mudslides that blocked roads, stranded travelers, and damaged buildings including a historic theater. In Telluride, muddy water and debris flooded classrooms at Telluride Intermediate School and sent nearly four feet of water into the Michael D. Palm Theatre, a landmark venue known for hosting the Telluride Film Festival.

At least three major mudslides struck East Portal Road and campground at Curecanti National Recreation Area near Gunnison on Thursday, temporarily stranding 21 people under up to six feet of boulders, rocks, and mud. The road passes through forest burned by the 2025 South Rim Fire, elevating flash flood and debris flow risk.

Additional slides swept over Highway 145, temporarily closing the busy road connecting Ridgway, Cortez, Telluride, and surrounding communities before it reopened Saturday night. National Weather Service meteorologist Cameron Simcoe warned that as the storm system moves northeast toward the Front Range and Eastern Plains this week, the combination of heavy rain and active wildfires will create ongoing hazards.

The rainfall has provided welcome drought relief in western Colorado, but forecasters cautioned that the slow pace of the storm elevated flooding risk. Residents are urged to monitor weather reports before venturing into backcountry or burn scar areas.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis' independent streak examined as tenure nears its end
stories.summary:
A retrospective analysis by the Colorado Springs Gazette examines how Gov. Jared Polis charted an independent course throughout his tenure, frequently diverging from national Democratic orthodoxy on issues ranging from pandemic policy to labor and his controversial commutation of Tina Peters' sentence. Polis, who became the nation's first openly gay governor and Colorado's first Jewish governor when elected in 2018, financed his campaigns largely with his own money, insulating him from party donor pressure.

Political science professors who spoke with the Gazette noted that Polis took a notably moderate pandemic approach compared to other Democratic governors, reopening parts of the economy earlier and stopping short of vaccine mandates, earning positive reviews from the business community. His pro-market posture extended across his tenure, though it drew criticism from progressives on issues including labor policy and the wolf reintroduction program.

The review comes as Polis prepares to leave office under term limits, with Attorney General Phil Weiser having won the Democratic gubernatorial primary. The Gazette notes that assessments of Polis' legacy vary sharply depending on political perspective, with some viewing his independence as principled governance and others seeing it as an obstacle to progressive priorities.

stories.headline: RTD faces calls to cut 16th Street shuttle as transit system struggles with post-pandemic ridership loss
stories.summary:
A Colorado Sun opinion column by veteran Denver journalist Diane Carman examines the crisis facing the Regional Transportation District, which has seen ridership remain roughly 40% below 2019 levels even as the agency has raised fares, cut staff, and reduced service. The piece takes direct aim at proposals to eliminate the 16th Street Mall shuttle, calling such discussions counterproductive even as it acknowledges RTD's systemic dysfunction.

RTD's troubles stem in part from the shift to remote work that decimated the commuter base the system was largely designed to serve, as well as ongoing funding and operational challenges that have compounded since the pandemic. The agency was built on a $4.7 billion voter-approved plan from 2004 to expand light rail and bus service across the region.

The column argues that cutting flagship services like the 16th Street shuttle would undermine the very urban core connectivity that makes transit viable for residents who rely on it for daily errands and grocery trips. The piece calls for a broader public reckoning with how Denver funds and structures its transit system in an era of changed commuting patterns.

stories.headline: Rockies' Hunter Goodman belts three home runs in loss to Reds, becomes first in franchise history with two three-homer games in a season
stories.summary:
Colorado Rockies catcher Hunter Goodman hit three home runs Sunday at Coors Field, driving in five runs in a 9-6 loss to the Cincinnati Reds. Goodman, who also hit three home runs against the Twins on June 27, became the first player in Rockies franchise history to accomplish the feat twice in the same season. He now has 30 home runs on the year, trailing only the Phillies' Kyle Schwarber (33) and the Astros' Yordan Alvarez (31) among major league leaders.

Only three other Colorado Rockies — Larry Walker, Todd Helton, and Carlos González — have recorded multiple three-homer games during their careers, putting Goodman in elite company. Walker, inducted into the Hall of Fame in 2021, accomplished it three times. Manager Warren Schaeffer noted that Goodman "looks dangerous" and praised his confidence and timing at the plate.

Despite Goodman's heroics, Cincinnati's Hunter Greene struck out seven over six innings to earn the win. The Reds built an 8-1 lead after two innings on homers from Nathaniel Lowe, Sal Stewart, and Tyler Stephenson, making a Colorado comeback impossible despite late home runs from Goodman and Willi Castro. The Rockies fell to 40-61 and open a three-game series Monday against the Washington Nationals.

stories.headline: Seahawks' $9.6 billion sale expected to dramatically boost Denver Broncos' franchise valuation
stories.summary:
The reported sale of the Seattle Seahawks to a group led by venture capitalist Vinod Khosla for a league-record $9.6 billion is expected to significantly increase the estimated value of the Denver Broncos, according to sports-business experts interviewed by The Denver Post. Forbes ranked both franchises nearly identically in 2025 — the Broncos at $6.8 billion (No. 13) and the Seahawks at $6.7 billion (No. 14) — making the Seahawks' sale price a direct market signal for Denver's worth.

Sports business expert Patrick Rishe, director of the sports business program at Washington University in St. Louis, told The Post that the Broncos' franchise value could now be "pretty close to $9.6 billion" based on the comparable market sizes and brand strengths of the two franchises. The Walton-Penner ownership group purchased the Broncos in 2022 for a then-record $4.65 billion, meaning the investment has seen substantial appreciation.

Experts noted the valuation could climb further if the Walton-Penner group succeeds in its plans to develop a new mixed-use stadium district at Burnham Yard in Denver, a project that could vault the franchise into the top tier of NFL valuations. The Seahawks' sale price was described by one economist as likely inflated by competitive bidding but nonetheless a meaningful reset for the broader market.

stories.headline: Denver makes case for 2031 Women's World Cup hosting bid on strength of NWSL success
stories.summary:
A Denver Gazette column argues that Denver should be a top candidate to host matches in the 2031 FIFA Women's World Cup, citing the booming success of Denver Summit FC in the NWSL and the city's demonstrated appetite for soccer. Summit FC entered Saturday averaging 28,436 fans per home match, the highest average in the NWSL, and opens its new home at Centennial Stadium this weekend against the Portland Thorns.

The column notes that Denver missed out on hosting matches for the 2026 Men's World Cup, whose final between Argentina and Spain drew enormous attention this weekend. Columnist Paul Klee argues the planned new Broncos stadium would provide an ideal international-caliber venue if the Women's World Cup bid is pursued.

The potential involvement of U.S. women's national team captain and Golden, Colorado native Lindsey Heaps adds further local narrative appeal to a potential hosting bid, the column notes. The 2031 Women's World Cup host selection process has not yet concluded.

stories.headline: Aurora's CommonGround Golf Course hosts caddie academy offering teens financial literacy, college scholarship path
stories.summary:
The Solich Caddie & Leadership Academy at Aurora's CommonGround Golf Course is training a new generation of young caddies, combining a well-paying summer job with financial literacy education and a path to the prestigious Evans Scholarship, which covers full college tuition and housing. The program, administered by the Colorado Golf Association, is named for Colorado energy entrepreneurs George and Geoff "Duffy" Solich and targets students starting in eighth grade who qualify by financial need and academic excellence.

Caddies in training can earn between $30 and $90 per loop depending on experience and course, with mentor-golfers volunteering to walk the course alongside them and teach the practical and interpersonal skills the job demands. Program participants and alumni describe the experience as a rare opportunity to develop confidence and adult communication skills outside of academic settings.

The Evans Scholarship has 1,260 recipients currently enrolled at 27 universities nationwide. CommonGround, a municipal course operated in Aurora, serves as one of the primary training grounds for the program in the Denver metro area.

stories.headline: New nonstop Denver-Ruidoso flight opens access to lesser-known New Mexico mountain getaway
stories.summary:
Contour Airlines has launched a new nonstop service between Denver and Sierra Blanca Regional Airport in Ruidoso, New Mexico, the airport's first commercial flight offering in more than 15 years. The route gives Colorado travelers direct access to a pine-shaded mountain community of 7,500 residents situated adjacent to the 1.1 million-acre Lincoln National Forest, offering hiking, fishing, mountain biking, and skiing at Ski Apache.

Ruidoso Mayor Lynn Crawford said the route benefits both communities, giving locals access to Denver's international connections while opening the town to Colorado tourists. The Denver Post travel feature highlights Grindstone Lake Recreation Area, White Sands National Park, and the town's arts scene as key draws.

Ruidoso has been ranked fifth in the nation for affordable astrotourism due to its minimal light pollution and proximity to dark-sky viewing areas. The route represents an expansion of Front Range travelers' options for mountain-town getaways beyond Colorado's borders.

stories.headline: Colorado author explores life of Sand Creek hero Silas Soule in new book
stories.summary:
Rev. Dr. Nancy Niero, a Denver-area United Church of Christ clergywoman and hospice chaplain, has published "Witness at Sand Creek, The Life and Letters of Silas Soule," a book examining the life of the Army officer who refused to participate in the 1864 Sand Creek Massacre and later testified against its perpetrators. The book, released through Exact Rush Publishing, draws on a collection of Soule's letters from 1860 to 1865 and incorporates Niero's own pilgrimages to sites where those letters were written.

Niero first encountered Soule's story as a museum educator at what is now History Colorado, where his portrait hung near the entrance alongside a tipi. She later pursued four independent studies on Soule while completing a Master of Divinity at Iliff School of Theology in Denver before ultimately writing the full biography.

The Sand Creek Massacre, in which Colorado militia attacked a peaceful encampment of Cheyenne and Arapaho people in southeastern Colorado, remains one of the most examined and contested events in Colorado history. Niero's work adds to a growing body of scholarship examining white witnesses and dissenters during the era.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My wife asked me to describe her in one word. I said, 'Terrifying.' I don't think she was expecting that, but I was being honest.

topStory.headline: U.S. and Iran Exchange Strikes After Three American Service Members Killed; Oil Tops $90 a Barrel
topStory.summary:
The United States launched a new round of airstrikes against Iran early Monday, targeting military command centers, air defense sites, coastal surveillance installations, maritime capabilities, and missile and drone launch sites, according to the U.S. military. The strikes came in retaliation for a series of Iranian attacks that killed three American service members and left one missing over the preceding days. President Donald Trump confirmed the offensive, saying, "We hit them very hard again tonight."

Two of the deaths occurred Friday and Saturday when Iranian missiles struck Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan. The Pentagon identified the fallen soldiers as First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, assigned to Fort Bragg, North Carolina, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, assigned to Ansbach, Germany. A third service member was killed Saturday in northern Iraq during a controlled detonation of an unexploded ordnance found on a downed Iranian attack drone, while a fourth soldier initially listed as missing was subsequently found, with the military reporting that unidentified remains were recovered Sunday and sent for examination.

Iran responded to the U.S. strikes by launching attacks against Bahrain, home to the U.S. Navy's Fifth Fleet, and Kuwait, while also striking tankers in the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian media reported U.S. hits near Tabriz, believed to house underground missile bases, and Bushehr, home to Iran's only operational civilian nuclear power plant. Benchmark Brent crude rose above $90 a barrel Monday, deepening a global energy crisis as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which previously carried roughly a fifth of global oil and gas supplies, has slowed to a trickle.

The escalating exchange has pushed both nations closer to all-out war despite an interim memorandum of understanding reached last month that was intended to halt attacks and open the strait over a 60-day implementation period. Mediators including Qatar and Pakistan have continued to engage both sides. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said Monday that diplomatic channels remained active, while U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Washington remained open to negotiations but that any talks "have to be real."
